text: The transformer architecture, introduced in the landmark 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' by Vaswani et al., fundamentally changed the landscape of natural language processing. At its core, the transformer replaces recurrent networks with a self-attention mechanism that allows each token to attend to all other tokens in the sequence simultaneously, enabling much greater parallelism during training. The architecture consists of stacked encoder and decoder blocks, each containing multi-head attention layers, position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections, and layer normalisation. This design made it possible to train on vastly larger datasets and scale model capacity in ways that were previously impractical.

Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has become a cornerstone technique for aligning large language models with human preferences. The process involves three stages: first, supervised fine-tuning on demonstration data; second, training a reward model on human preference comparisons; and third, optimising the language model using proximal policy optimisation (PPO) to maximise the learned reward signal. This pipeline steers the model toward responses that are helpful, harmless, and honest. Variants such as Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) have since emerged that bypass the explicit reward model, simplifying the training pipeline while achieving comparable alignment results.

Data quality in large-scale machine learning is a multi-dimensional challenge that encompasses correctness, completeness, consistency, and freshness. Poor data quality is frequently the root cause of model failures that appear to be architectural or algorithmic in origin. Production ML teams invest heavily in systematic quality evaluation pipelines that combine fast heuristic filters, semantic deduplication, and LLM-based scoring to ensure training corpora meet rigorous standards before any compute is spent on model training. The cost of training a frontier model makes pre-training data curation one of the highest-leverage activities in the entire ML development lifecycle.

Semantic deduplication is the process of identifying and removing documents that are near-identical in meaning, even when their surface text differs. Unlike exact deduplication, which relies on hash matching, semantic dedup uses embedding similarity or Jaccard similarity over character or word n-gram shingles. The MinHash algorithm provides a space-efficient approximation of Jaccard similarity and, combined with locality-sensitive hashing (LSH), enables sub-linear query time even for corpora with hundreds of millions of documents. Studies have shown that aggressive deduplication consistently improves downstream model performance, likely because it reduces the model's tendency to memorise repeated content.

The scaling laws for neural language models, characterised empirically by Kaplan et al. (2020) and later refined by Hoffmann et al. (2022) in the Chinchilla paper, describe how model loss decreases predictably as a power law function of model size, dataset size, and compute budget. The Chinchilla analysis found that most prior large models were significantly undertrained relative to their parameter count — that is, more training tokens would have been more compute-efficient than adding more parameters. These findings have reshaped the strategy for frontier model training, with modern labs allocating significantly more tokens per parameter than earlier practice dictated.

La intelligence artificielle transforme rapidement le monde du travail et de la recherche. Les modèles de langage de grande taille, tels que GPT-4 et Claude, sont capables de raisonner sur des problèmes complexes, de générer du code et d'assister les chercheurs dans leurs travaux. Cette évolution soulève des questions importantes sur l'avenir de l'emploi, la sécurité des systèmes et l'éthique de l'intelligence artificielle.

Constitutional AI, introduced by Anthropic, is an approach to training AI systems to be helpful, harmless, and honest by having the model critique and revise its own outputs based on a set of principles — the 'constitution'. During supervised learning, the model is trained on data generated by having it self-critique against the constitution. During RL, a feedback model trained on these critiques replaces human raters, enabling scalable alignment without requiring human labelling of every potentially harmful output. This approach significantly reduces the need for human feedback while maintaining strong safety properties.

The transformer architecture, introduced in the landmark 2017 paper Attention Is All You Need by Vaswani et al., fundamentally changed the landscape of natural language processing. At its core, the transformer replaces recurrent networks with a self-attention mechanism that allows each token to attend to all other tokens simultaneously. The architecture consists of stacked encoder and decoder blocks, enabling much greater parallelism during training.

Mixture of Experts (MoE) is a neural network architecture in which a learned routing mechanism selects a subset of specialised sub-networks — the 'experts' — to process each input token. By activating only a fraction of the total parameters per forward pass, MoE models achieve much larger effective capacity without a proportional increase in compute. GPT-4, Mixtral, and Grok-1 are believed to use MoE architectures. The key challenge in MoE training is load balancing: if the router collapses to always selecting the same experts, most capacity is wasted. Auxiliary losses and careful initialisation are used to encourage uniform expert utilisation.

Prompt engineering has emerged as a practical skill for eliciting high-quality outputs from large language models without changing model weights. Key techniques include chain-of-thought prompting, which encourages the model to reason step by step; few-shot prompting, which provides examples of desired input-output pairs; role-playing, which frames the model's task as adopting a particular persona; and structured output prompting, which constrains responses to JSON or other machine-readable formats. As models grow more capable, effective prompting increasingly involves understanding the model's training distribution and the subtle ways in which phrasing affects output quality.

Fine-tuning a pretrained language model on a downstream task has become the dominant paradigm in NLP. Rather than training from scratch, practitioners start from a model pretrained on a large general corpus and continue training on task-specific labelled data. This approach is dramatically more sample-efficient than training from scratch because the pretrained model already encodes rich linguistic and world knowledge. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods such as LoRA, QLoRA, and prefix tuning reduce the number of trainable parameters further, making it feasible to adapt large models on consumer hardware.

Evaluating the capabilities and limitations of large language models is one of the most active and contentious areas of AI research. Benchmarks such as MMLU, BIG-Bench, HumanEval, and MATH attempt to measure reasoning, knowledge, coding ability, and mathematical problem-solving. However, there is ongoing concern that frontier models may overfit to benchmark distributions during training, making benchmark scores an unreliable proxy for true capability. The field is moving toward more diverse evaluation suites, red-teaming, and task-specific evaluations that are harder to saturate.

Speculative decoding is a technique to accelerate autoregressive inference in large language models by using a smaller, faster draft model to propose several candidate tokens at once, which the larger target model then verifies in a single forward pass. Because verification can be done in parallel, this approach achieves substantial speedups — often 2–3x — without changing the output distribution of the target model. The draft model must be aligned closely enough with the target that most proposed tokens are accepted; otherwise the overhead of rejection and re-sampling degrades performance.

The transformer architecture, introduced in the landmark 2017 paper Attention Is All You Need by Vaswani et al., changed NLP. The transformer replaces recurrent networks with self-attention, allowing tokens to attend to all others. It has stacked encoder and decoder blocks with multi-head attention and feed-forward layers.
